narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, an LSAT score of 28, middle-family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school deciles from Year 1 and Year 3 both sitting at the 2nd decile—meaning your early grades placed you in the lower end compared to your peers, well below the typical 6th decile averages. The admissions model, trained on thousands of similar students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 94% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk, even though your undergrad GPA was close to the average of 3.21. The three biggest factors pushing this prediction toward failure were, in order of strongest influence, your Year 3 school decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile—all of which worked against you by signaling lower academic readiness relative to what the model associates with bar success. Specifically, your low Year 3 decile had the most weight, as it suggested your performance didn't improve over time like it does for many who pass the bar. Your LSAT score, far below the dataset average of about 37, reinforced this by indicating potential challenges with the rigorous reasoning needed for law exams. Similarly, the low Year 1 decile set an early concerning tone, deviating from the stronger starts typical of bar passers. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of students (over 90%) and didn't sway things much, these academic markers dominated the decision. Compared to typical admitted students, your metrics fell short in these key areas, placing you outside the profiles that usually predict passing. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep programs tailored for Hispanic students aiming for law, and reapplying next cycle with those improvements—many have turned similar situations around by focusing on these exact gaps. This rejection isn't a reflection of your potential as a Hispanic leader in law, but a data-driven flag to target these hurdles head-on. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile into the successful range.
